author: @IslandHermit
text:
The problem I have with that style of heater is that the core is so dense that the tiny fan is utterly inadequate to the task of pushing air through it. Heat quickly builds up in and around the case, triggering the thermostat. As a result the thermostat is all but useless.

I'd add one caveat to your point about all heaters being the same in terms of efficiency. So-called "radiant" heaters (all heaters are radiant, but that's another discussion) can be aimed to deliver a significant portion of their heat to a specific location in a room, e.g. the chair you sit in to write YouTube comments. That way you can create a zone of warmth where you most need it without having to get the entire room up to a comfortable temperature.

author: @lisakingscott7729
text: I think that device is probably illegal in the UK due to no fuse in the plug. BS1363, the standard which UK mains plugs must adhere to, specifies that a BS1362 fuse must be fitted. The UK is unusual around the world in requiring fused plugs, but most people don't understand why. They are there primarily to protect the house wiring due to the UK's unusual use of ring mains.

author: @lisakingscott7729
text: I understand the physics, but it's probably a good idea to explain to the less well educated something about insulation and heating. I.e. as you said, the time taken to heat the room will depend on the insulation. However the flow of energy through the insulation, at a given delta T may exceed the capability of the heater. Therefore your statement that a 500W heater will take 10 times as long to heat a room, is not necessarily correct and depends on the insulation. In the UK most homes are really badly insulated and I suspect 500W will not adequately heat a room in many houses with zub zero outside temperature.

Anyway, thanks for sharing your thoughts on the matter...

text: Brilliant video Robert and well explained. When I commented on your previous video regarding these things, I was focused in my mind how they were being mis sold as some sort of heating miracle. Seeing you break that one down was quite worrying, to see the poor build quality and lack of protection for something that is going to be plugged in to a ring main circuit rated at 30amps or so. A fire just waiting to happen. I guess they'd be quite an effective "heater" at that point. Thanks for taking the time to make this video. You've proven the point superbly 👍👍

author: @acada6594
text: About the heating. It is not all about the power. You need the heat distribution. 10kW does not you any good if the heat is on one spot in the room. Plus there are other ways how to heat room. IR radiation can go through the air without raising its temperature. Then it can hit surface, which raises its temperature and then it passes its heat to the air. And it helps the heat distribution as well.

replies.author: @gerryheynes5082
replies.text: I always thought that too. 100w x 6 hours on low setting = 600w compared to 2.4kw oven on for say 50 minutes: max power for 10 or so minutes to heat up = 400w (if my maths or correct?) then say 40 minutes cooking cycling on and off - would that be more than 200w ? I’d think so, but the difference isn’t perhaps as great as people believe. I haven’t included that the slow cooker has done the veg too, which would probably go on the hob with the oven meal. What about microwaving the meal: 1300w for perhaps 15-18 minutes ... 380w ! With the price of food and energy though, it’s cheaper to go down the pub 😂

author: @TrumpstienPhile
text:
Worth noting rapidly heating a room is far more 'efficient'

 - heating  a room via convection at 500 watts means that the slightest draft will vent that heat to outside

Rapidly heating a room allows the heat to " soak into" walls and surfaces  so they store and slowly radiate the heat

replies.author: @philware1546
replies.text: Surely rapid and "soak in" are completely opposites.

author: @woodworks2123
text: Desert sun on YouTube did a great video of a 12v PTC  heater, a computer fan and connected directly to a 100 watt solar panel then 2 solar panels. The only problem with whichever electric heater you use, comes when there's no kind of thermostat, so if you can't set it to 20 degrees say, it just blasts out heat till you switch it off then in those cheaper models, if you just have it on for 8hrs then a 1kw  will obviously cost half as much as a 2kw. The take home is really use whichever heater you want but use one with a reliable room thermostat. As a side note, I experimented with those exact heaters last winter, it couldn't heat my livingroom to a comfortable level, there was too much heat loss.2 could but my small 2mx3m room 1 heated it with ease. Use a decent thermostat is the key. If you want something different, think about the problem differently. I'm playing with an instant water heater, 3kw. As my central heating pump has a flow of just 1litre per minute, this little 3kw water heater can supply that with ease. At the moment it's an open end after the radiators and is being drained but if I can close the loop it'll use less power as the waters not cold to start and it'll cost atleast 1/3rd of using my old gas boiler

author: @jasonburguess
text: Here in the US the lack of safety requirements for electrical appliances would amaze anyone from the UK, we have 120 vac at 20 amps in every outlet and separate 240vac at 60amps for big appliances like cooking ranges and washing machines etc. Almost nothing you buy has a fuse in it and they'll sell you an extension cord that will just melt if you draw the normal current through it. Thought you might find that funny

replies.author: @corringhamdepot4434
replies.text: I watched an American video comparing heaters in US stores described as "small room", "large room" etc. Except that they were all rated at 1500w. Which he explained as the maximum allowed in the US. So that using multiple heaters wouldn't overload your home circuits. Now we are getting sold the same heaters heaters here in the UK, instead of our old standard heaters with two 1KW bars.  The standard radiant heaters I see in our stores now, come with 3 X 400w "halogen" bars instead.

replies.author: @jasonburguess
replies.text:
@corringhamdepot4434 you should see the fraudulent claims on others us products, they have wet/dry vacuums that claim to be 4hp when it's impossible to draw more than 3/4 ho from a 1500watt outlet, it's just corporations capitalizing on stupidity, which is rampant in the US, I'd be very surprised if a college graduate from the USA could pass primary school tests for the UK
 We are the dumbest country in the developed world.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I do mate - I find that very odd

author: @randyfishbrews
text: You might as well use a hair dryer !!  and although it's all plastic I had to laugh when I saw the earth pin on the plug - it's not used of course but is insulated half way up the pin  -  I have never seen that on an official  UK  plug . 😀

replies.author: @stephenbrough8132
replies.text: It may have the "DOUBLE INSULATED" SYMBOL ON IT (A BOX WITHIN A BOX MEANS DOUBLE INSULATED - NO EARTH NEEDED)

replies.author: @randyfishbrews
replies.text: @stephenbrough8132 What?  I was talking about the earth pin on the plug !!  Nothing about a double insulated symbol ha ha

replies.author: @Fishing4Ideas
replies.text: @stephenbrough8132 he was saying the plug earth pin.  It's a classic thing that comes from a Chinese factory  - an insulated earth post on the plug  - watch the video again.....

replies.author: @sharkster1969
replies.text: @stephenbrough8132 You didnt understand what Randyfish was explaining.   It's the earth pin on the plug that doesnt need to be insulated half way up the pin!!

replies.author: @Talevori
replies.text: Yes I noticed that  lol you are spot on mate .    there is  no need to have a pvc safety shield on the earth pin of the mains plug it's a classic chinese thing

author: @kevgermany
text:
No fuses in Schuko plugs... But decent circuits, no dangerous ring mains. Continental standards rock. We even have safe 230V in the bathroom. That said, the lack of isolation and thermal cut out/fuse are distinctly worrying. I wouldn't.have one. 
But you overlook something here. Heating will be dependant on the temperature of the air moving through.  So a de ent cold airflow will probably push it over the paltry 500W output.

author: @Electrowave
text: My 500W ceramic heater was £9.99 from Home Bargains. It has a thermostat variable temperature and a lead with a fused plug. It looks the same as yours inside. Works great for my little bathroom :-)  (Don't attempt anything I do here unless you're a qualified electrician)

replies.author: @stephenbrough8132
replies.text: UK?   eRRR... although it's been a while since I consulted the IEE regulations on electrics in bathrooms,  I'm PRETTY sure you're not supposed to have that kind of appliance in a bathroom, mate ... Not tryna pee on your bonfire or anything - but in the UK, the regs have always made it pretty clear that we shouldn;t be bringing these kind of appliance into a bathroom - because of the real danger of electrocution being SO much greater when we are wet ... I think you REALLY need a proper FIXED appliance, designed for bathroom use, installed by a qualified electrician, up to speed with current regs - I;m a bit behind on the latest edition.     Just thinking of your safety, and that of anyone else who copies and thinks it;s OK to bring any appliance they want into a bathroom.     Electricity is very unforgiving.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: @stephenbrough8132 😂 do you seriously have laws that require these sorts of things?! 😂

replies.author: @scotts.2624
replies.text: @stephenbrough8132 I use a stand along electric heater in a bathroom.. Its not close enough to the tub to touch. Just because you are wet does not mean you will see current. The only time current can flow is if you are the path to ground. I could pick the thing up if I am dripping wet and as long as I am not grounded no current will flow.

replies.author: @teddycunningham7568
replies.text: Ah. Just make sure the cord is short so no one can throw it in the tub with you.

replies.author: @triedzidono
replies.text: @stephenbrough8132 WELL SAID !    further more, it is in fact illegal (& for good reason to those who value family members) to have any wall sockets at all installed in a bathroom besides the one you might plug a shaver into. Up high & isolated to IP44

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
I do like them for heating very small spaces (bathroom, closet just big enough to be converted as an office...) but, thank you very much, I'll be ditching that stupid power supply, DC fan, brainbox and display while adding two mains-voltage fans of different manufacturer (the typical failure-mode which results in the ceramic overheating and cracking) a resetting thermal cutout, a fusible thermal cutout a few degrees higher and overcurrent protection. 

I couldn't find the exact listing but I got a few several years ago from eBay. Literally nothing but the PTC device, a fan and a neon lamp with a case to go around it. All the additions I made came from scavenged components (computer power supplies, coffee makers, broken heaters...) I think it was about $10US shipped for each and I had some fun fixing the poor design.

Thing is, with that reduces BOM, it should be much less expensive to make. I suppose people have come to expect fancy lights, shiny displays and for the thing to die within a season or two.
